patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/4] Demotion cleanup and fixes

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/4] mm: Move demotion related functions in memory-tiers.c
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves functions related to demoting memory usage in the Linux kernel from mm/vmscan.c to mm/memory-tiers.c, as per Alexandre Ghiti's suggestion. The goal is to centralize these functions in a single file for better organization and maintainability. No functional changes are intended by this move.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Joshua Hahn suggested moving a helpful comment from vmscan.c to memory-tiers.c, noting that it clarifies the behavior of folios in @demote_folios when not demoted.

Reviewer noted that mt_demote_folios() function is not properly protected by locks and may lead to data corruption or other issues, but the rest of the patch looks good.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Alexandre,

I hope you are doing well! Thank you for the patch.

Makes sense to move the migration functions together. Just one small
nit, I think the following comment is pretty helpful in understanding
that folios that aren't demoted still remain in @demote_folios. Should
we also move this comment to memory-tiers.c?

[...snip...]

---

[...snip...]

Anyways, the rest looks good to me. Have a great day!
Joshua

review_comments.author: Donet Tom
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Donet Tom noted that the mt_demote_folios function is not properly handling the case where a folio is already being demoted, and requested that the function be modified to handle this scenario.

Reviewer questioned the necessity of the 'mt_' prefix in the function name mt_demote_folios(), suggesting it may be redundant since demotion already implies a change to a lower memory tier.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Alexander

On 3/11/26 4:32 PM, Alexandre Ghiti wrote:

---

Demotion will happen only when different memory tiers are present, 
right? Since demote_folios() already implies that the folios are being 
demoted to a lower tier, is the mt_ prefix needed in the function name? 
I\u2019m fine with keeping it as is, but I just wanted to clarify.

Otherwise it LGTM

Reviewed by: Donet Tom <donettom@linux.ibm.com>

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer David (Arm) requested consistent indentation in memory-tiers.c, specifically two-tab indentation for the second parameter line in several functions.

Reviewer David (Arm) questioned whether the patch was based on an outdated version of the code, specifically mentioning that the function mt_demote_folios() has changed in mm/mm-unstable.
review_comments.raw_body:
use two-tab indentation on second parameter line please. So this fits
into a single line. Same for the other functions.

Just like alloc_demote_folio() that you are moving already did.

[...]

---

I think this function changed in the meantime in mm/mm-unstable. Against
which branch is this patch?

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Alexandre Ghiti (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2.

Author acknowledged a potential inconsistency in the naming convention for functions in memory-tiers.c and decided to keep the mt_ prefix despite reviewer's suggestion.

Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Tom,

On 3/12/26 09:44, Donet Tom wrote:

---

You're right, demote implies some memory tiers. But I like the mt_ 
prefix, some functions in memory-tiers.c already have this prefix so it 
adds consistency: so since you don't mind, I'll keep it :)

---

Thanks for your time!

Alex

review_comments.author: Alexandre Ghiti (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2.

Alex

review_comments.summary:

Author acknowledged that the patch was not rebased against Linus v7.0-rc3 and promised to rebase it in a future version (v2).

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/4] mm: Rename node_get_allowed_targets() to make it more explicit
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the function `node_get_allowed_targets()` to `node_get_allowed_demotion_targets()` in the Linux kernel's memory management code. The original function name was misleading, as it actually returns tier nodes targeted during demotion, not allowed targets. This change aims to make the function name more explicit and accurate. No functional changes are intended by this patch.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary: Reviewer suggested renaming node_get_allowed_targets() to something more explicit and agreed with the original patch, but thought the new name could be shortened further.
review_comments.raw_body:
Agreed, node_get_allowed_targets is pretty vague ; -)

I do think that node_get_allowed_demotion_targets could be considered
a bit too long, but I don't think it's called in too many places where
there would be more than just this function call in the line so LGTM!

Please feel free to add my review tag, have a great day Alexandre!

Reviewed-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>

review_comments.author: Byungchul Park
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Byungchul Park suggested renaming the function to node_get_demotion_targets() instead of node_get_allowed_demotion_targets(), finding it more intuitive.
review_comments.raw_body:
Look better than before to me.

What about just node_get_demotion_targets()?

	Byungchul

review_comments.author: Donet Tom
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Donet Tom approved the patch, giving it a 'Reviewed-by' tag.
review_comments.raw_body:
This looks LGTM

Reviewed by: Donet Tom <donettom@linux.ibm.com>

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David suggested renaming node_get_allowed_demotion_targets() to mt_get_node_demotion_targets() for consistency with other functions in the memory-tiers API, and gave a positive review (+1) indicating approval of the patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
+1

Maybe throw in the mt_ prefix and call it

mt_get_node_demotion_targets()

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Alexandre Ghiti (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the function name change was not properly reflected in all instances and agreed to restructure in v2.

Author agreed to rename the function in a future version of the patch, specifically v2.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi David, Byungchul,

On 3/12/26 13:58, David Hildenbrand (Arm) wrote:

---

I'll do, thanks

Alex

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/4] mm: Fix demotion gfp by clearing GFP_RECLAIM after setting GFP_TRANSHUGE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a demotion issue in the Linux kernel's memory management system by clearing GFP_RECLAIM after setting GFP_TRANSHUGE. The problem was caused by an incorrect ordering of these flags, which led to inconsistent behavior between regular and transparent huge page (THP) allocations. The fix involves simply swapping the order of operations, ensuring that GFP_RECLAIM is cleared before GFP_TRANSHUGE is set.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Andrew Morton requested separation of stable fixes from cleanups and preparation against current mainline, as well as careful changelog description of userspace-visible runtime effects.
review_comments.raw_body:
Please let's have the cc:stable fixes separated out from the cleanups,
and prepared against current -linus mainline.

Also, when proposing backportable fixes please ensure that the
changelogs carefully describe the userspace-visible runtime effects of
the bug.

Thanks.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Johannes Weiner noted that the original patch's fix inadvertently disabled direct reclaim for THP allocations, making the request unnecessarily wimpy, and suggested a clearer comment to explain GFP_TRANSHUGE's own reclaim policy.
review_comments.raw_body:
I don't think this is right.

The Fixes: did it this way to disable kswapd for THP allocations,
while still allowing the customary direct reclaim. Maybe a better
comment would have been: /* GFP_TRANSHUGE has its own reclaim policy */

After your fix, direct reclaim isn't allowed either, which makes the
request unnecessarily wimpy.

The Closes: refers to reclaim that should be avoided during demotion.
But if this path is taken during demotion it will already not recurse
into direct reclaim due to PF_MEMALLOC.

So I don't see a bug in the existing code. But maybe the comment could
be clearer.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David requested that fixes be moved to the top of the patchset or sent independently
review_comments.raw_body:
Also, please move fixes to the very beginning of the patch set, or
better, send them independently.

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Gregory Price noted that the GFP_RECLAIM flag is being masked and added back in multiple layers, making it difficult to reason about what the actual flags are at any given point.
review_comments.raw_body:
The bigger issue how many times we see this particular flag getting
masked and apparently added back in at multiple layers. We saw two
or three paths (some unreachable) that can twiddle RECLAIM flags in
the stack for demotion (which is in reclaim already, so do the flags
matter?).

It makes it difficult to reason about what the GFP flags actually
are at any given point.

But yeah I wasn't sure to make of this code, it could be as you
suggested just a bad comment.

~Gregory

review_comments.author: Alexandre Ghiti (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that GFP_RECLAIM should be cleared after setting GFP_TRANSHUGE to maintain consistency with regular THP allocations and agreed to restructure in a future version (v2) to address the issue.

Author acknowledged a fix is needed to clear GFP_RECLAIM after setting GFP_TRANSHUGE and agreed to make the change.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Andrew,

On 3/11/26 18:06, Andrew Morton wrote:

---

I was unaware of that requirement, I'll do.

Thanks,

Alex

review_comments.author: Alexandre Ghiti (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the GFP_RECLAIM flag must be cleared after setting GFP_TRANSHUGE to avoid inconsistent behavior, and agreed to restructure in a future version.

Author acknowledged that they misunderstood the original comment and agreed to restructure the patch in a future version.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Johannes,

On 3/11/26 18:54, Johannes Weiner wrote:

---

Makes sense, I had not understood the comment indeed. I'll drop this fix 
in the next version then.

Thanks,

Alex

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/4] mm: Fix demotion gfp by preserving initial gfp reclaim policy
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a demotion gfp issue in the Linux kernel's memory management subsystem, specifically when handling huge pages and migration targets. The problem arises from the unconditional enabling of reclaim during demotion, which can lead to premature folio refcount drops. To fix this, the initial GFP flags are preserved, preventing direct reclaim.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with the patch's approach to preserving initial GFP flags. Suggested an alternative solution using a different flag combination.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series cleans up and fixes demotion-related functions in the Linux kernel. It moves related functions to a new file, renames some functions for clarity, and corrects issues with GFP flags used during demotion. The changes aim to improve the memory management system by making it more explicit and efficient.

I don't think this is right.

The Fixes: did it this way to disable kswapd for THP allocations,

comment would have been: /* GFP_TRANSHUGE has its own reclaim policy */

request unnecessarily wimpy.

The Closes: refers to reclaim that should be avoided during demotion.
But if this path is taken during demotion it will already not recurse
into direct reclaim due to PF_MEMALLOC.

So I don't see a bug in the existing code. But maybe the comment could
be clearer.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: btrfs_log_dev_io_error() on all bio errors

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: btrfs_log_dev_io_error() on all bio errors

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the btrfs_log_dev_io_error() function to log device I/O errors for all bio error status codes, not just BLK_STS_IOERR and BLK_STS_TARGET. This change aims to prevent incorrect conclusions about system behavior when encountering different error codes from the block layer.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with a Reviewed-by tag, indicating it meets kernel coding standards.
review_comments.raw_body:
\u5728 2026/3/14 05:56, Boris Burkov \u5199\u9053:
> As far as I can tell, we never intentionally constrained ourselves to
> these status codes, and it is misleading and surprising to lack the
> bdev error logging when we get a different error code from the block
> layer. This can lead to jumping to a wrong conclusion like "this
> system didn't see any bio failures but aborted with EIO".
> 
> For example on nvme devices, I observe many failures coming back as
> BLK_STS_MEDIUM. It is apparent that the nvme driver returns a variety of
> BLK_STS_* status values in nvme_error_status().
> 
> Signed-off-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>

Reviewed-by: Qu Wenruo <wqu@suse.com>

Thanks,
Qu


> ---
>   fs/btrfs/bio.c | 3 +--
>   1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 2 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/bio.c b/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> index 2a2a21aec817..08b1d62603d0 100644
> --- a/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> +++ b/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> @@ -352,8 +352,7 @@ static void btrfs_log_dev_io_error(const struct bio *bio, struct btrfs_device *d
>   {
>   	if (!dev || !dev->bdev)
>   		return;
> -	if (bio->bi_status != BLK_STS_IOERR && bio->bi_status != BLK_STS_TARGET)
> -		return;
> +	ASSERT(bio->bi_status);
>   
>   	if (btrfs_op(bio) == BTRFS_MAP_WRITE)
>   		btrfs_dev_stat_inc_and_print(dev, BTRFS_DEV_STAT_WRITE_ERRS);

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH v3] btrfs: fix a crash in the trace event btrfs_sync_file()
